title: Equivalent ratio word problems
description: Practice solving ratio word problems like, "If Ben reads 10 pages in 15 minutes, how long does it take him to read 40 pages?"
topic_path: Topic A: Representing and reasoning about ratios

assessment_items.question_content:
Amara needs $90$ kilograms of meat to feed her $4$ pet dragons each day. Each pet dragon eats the same amount of meat.

 **How many kilograms of meat does Amara need to feed $2$ pet dragons each day?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] kilograms
hints:
Each dragon eats the same amount. 

So an equivalent ratio can show us how much to feed $2$ dragons.

To make an equivalent ratio, we multiply or divide both values in the first ratio by the same number.

|||
-|-|-|-
Dragons | $4$ | $\rightarrow$ | $2$
Meat ($\text{kg}$) | $90$ | $\rightarrow$  | $?$

|||
-|-|-|-
Dragons | $4$ | $\stackrel{\times \purpleD{\dfrac{1}{2}}}\rightarrow$ | $2$
Meat ($\text{kg}$) | $90$ | $\stackrel{\times \purpleD{\dfrac{1}{2}}}\rightarrow$ | $\tealE{45}$

Amara needs to feed $\purpleD{\dfrac{1}{2}}$ as many pet dragons, so she needs $\purpleD{\dfrac{1}{2}}$ as much meat.
Amara needs $\tealE{45}$ kilograms of meat to feed $2$ pet dragons each day.

assessment_items.question_content:
Omar has a new debit card. He earns $1{,}800$ reward points for spending $300$ dollars. 

 **At this rate, if Omar earned $600$ reward points, how much money did he spend?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] dollars
hints:
Omar earns points at the same rate for each dollar.

So an equivalent ratio can show us how many dollars Omar spends to earn $600$ points.

To make an equivalent ratio, we multiply or divide both values in the first ratio by the same number.

|||
-|-|-|-
Points | $1{,}800$ | $\rightarrow$ | $600$
Dollars | $300$ | $\rightarrow$  | $?$

|||
-|-|-|-
Points | $1{,}800$ | $\stackrel{\times \purpleD{\dfrac{1}{3}}}\rightarrow$ | $600$
Dollars | $300$ | $\stackrel{\times \purpleD{\dfrac{1}{3}}}\rightarrow$  | $\tealE{100}$

Omar earned $\purpleD{\dfrac{1}{3}}$ times as many points, so he spent $\purpleD{\dfrac{1}{3}}$ times as much money.
Omar spent $\tealE{100}$ dollars to earn $600$ points.

assessment_items.question_content:
Laine reads $200$ pages in $240$ minutes.

 **If Laine reads $25$ pages at this same rate, how long will it take her?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] minutes
hints:
Laine reads each page at the same rate.

So an equivalent ratio can show us how long it will take to read $25$ pages.

To make an equivalent ratio, we multiply or divide both values in the first ratio by the same number.

|||
-|-|-|-
Pages | $200$ | $\rightarrow$ | $25$
Minutes | $240$ | $\rightarrow$  | $?$

|||
-|-|-|-
Pages | $200$ | $\stackrel{\times \purpleD{\dfrac{1}{8}}}\rightarrow$ | $25$
Minutes | $240$ | $\stackrel{\times \purpleD{\dfrac{1}{8}}}\rightarrow$  | $\tealE{30}$

Laine is reading $\purpleD{\dfrac{1}{8}}$ times as many pages, so reading will take $\purpleD{\dfrac{1}{8}}$ times as many minutes.
It will take Laine $\tealE{30}$ minutes to read $25$ pages.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ben the camel drinks tea (so classy!). He drinks $1{,}050$ liters of tea every $6$ days. Ben drinks the same amount each day.

 **How many liters of tea does Ben drink every $2$ days?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] liters
hints:
Ben drinks the same amount each day.

So an equivalent ratio can show us how much he drinks in $2$ days.

To make an equivalent ratio, we multiply or divide both values in the first ratio by the same number.

|||
-|-|-|-
Days | $6$ | $\rightarrow$ | $2$
Liters of tea | $1{,}050$ | $\rightarrow$  | $?$

|||
-|-|-|-
Days | $6$ | $\stackrel{\times \purpleD{\dfrac{1}{3}}}\rightarrow$ | $2$
Liters of tea | $1{,}050$ | $\stackrel{\times \purpleD{\dfrac{1}{3}}}\rightarrow$  | $\redE{350}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

There are $\purpleD{\dfrac{1}{3}}$ times as many days, so Ben will drink $\purpleD{\dfrac{1}{3}}$ times as much tea.
Ben drinks $\redE{350}$ liters of tea every $2$ days.

assessment_items.question_content:
A fruit basket is filled with $8$ bananas, $3$ oranges, $5$ apples, and $6$ kiwis.

**Complete the ratio.**

**For every $3$ kiwis, there are $4$ [[☃ dropdown 1]].**
hints:
We are looking for a ratio of kiwis: another fruit.

We need to be able to simplify the ratio to $3:4$.

The total number of kiwis in the ratio has been simplified by $\purpleE{\text{dividing by 2}}$.

|Total||Ratio part
-|-|-|-
Kiwis | $6$ | $\stackrel{\purpleE{\div 2}}\rightarrow$ | $3$
? fruit | $\blueE{?}$ | $\stackrel{\purpleE{\div 2}}\rightarrow$ | $\maroonD{4}$

So, we are looking for a $\blueE{\text{number of fruit}}$  that we can also $\purpleE{\text{divide by 2}}$ to get $\maroonD4$.

|Total||Ratio part
-|-|-|-
Kiwis | $6$ | $\stackrel{\purpleE{\div 2}}\rightarrow$ | $3$
? fruit | $\blueE{8}$ | $\stackrel{\purpleE{\div 2}}\rightarrow$ | $\maroonD{4}$

$\blueE{8} \purpleE{\div 2} = \maroonD4$

What fruit has a total of $8$?
For every $3$ kiwis, there are $4$ bananas.

assessment_items.question_content:
Sunny's garden has $9$ tomato plants,  $5$ pepper plants, $3$ squash plants, and $6$ pumpkin plants. 

**Complete the ratio.**

**For every $3$ tomato plants in Sunny's garden, there is $1$ [[☃ dropdown 1]].**
hints:
We are looking for a ratio of tomato plants: $\_\_\_$ plants.

We need to be able to simplify the ratio to $3:1$.

The total number of tomato plants in the ratio has been simplified by $\purpleE{\text{dividing by 3}}$.

|Total||Ratio part
-|-|-|-
Tomato plants | $9$ | $\stackrel{\purpleE{\div 3}}\rightarrow$ | $3$
? plants | $\blueE{?}$ | $\stackrel{\purpleE{\div 3}}\rightarrow$ | $\maroonD{1}$

So, we are looking for a $\blueE{\text{total number of plants}}$  that we can also $\purpleE{\text{divide by 3}}$ to get $\maroonD1$.

|Total||Ratio part
-|-|-|-
Tomato plants | $9$ | $\stackrel{\purpleE{\div 3}}\rightarrow$ | $3$
? plants | $\blueE{3}$ | $\stackrel{\purpleE{\div 3}}\rightarrow$ | $\maroonD{1}$

$\blueE3 \purpleE{\div 3} = \maroonD1$

What plant has a total of $3$ plants?
For every $3$ tomato plants in Sunny's garden, there is $1$ squash plant.

assessment_items.question_content:
A bike shop has $11$ red bikes,  $3$ blue bikes, $4$ orange bikes, and $12$ silver bikes. 

**Complete the ratio.**

**For every $1$ orange bike, there are $3$ [[☃ dropdown 1]].**
hints:
We are looking for a ratio of orange bikes: $\_\_\_$ bikes.

We need to be able to simplify the ratio to $1:3$.

The total number of orange bikes in the ratio has been simplified by $\purpleE{\text{dividing by 4}}$.

|Total||Ratio part
-|-|-|-
Orange bikes | $4$ | $\stackrel{\purpleE{\div 4}}\rightarrow$ | $1$
? bikes | $\blueE{?}$ | $\stackrel{\purpleE{\div 4}}\rightarrow$ | $\maroonD{3}$

So, we are looking for a $\blueE{\text{total number of bikes}}$  that we can also $\purpleE{\text{divide by 4}}$ to get $\maroonD3$.

|Total||Ratio part
-|-|-|-
Orange bikes | $4$ | $\stackrel{\purpleE{\div 4}}\rightarrow$ | $1$
? bikes | $\blueE{12}$ | $\stackrel{\purpleE{\div 4}}\rightarrow$ | $\maroonD{3}$

$\blueE{12} \purpleE{\div 4} = \maroonD3$

What color has a total of $12$ bikes?
For every $1$ orange bike, there are $3$ silver bikes.

assessment_items.question_content:
A pet groomer gave $9$ baths, $15$ nail trims, $8$ brush-outs, and $5$ ear cleanings.

**For every $5$ nail trims, the groomer gave $3$ [[☃ dropdown 1]].**
hints:
We are looking for a ratio of nail trims: another service.

We need to be able to simplify the ratio to $5:3$.

The total number of nail trims in the ratio has been simplified by $\purpleE{\text{dividing by 3}}$.

|Total||Ratio part
-|-|-|-
Nail trims | $15$ | $\stackrel{\purpleE{\div 3}}\rightarrow$ | $5$
? service | $\blueE{?}$ | $\stackrel{\purpleE{\div 3}}\rightarrow$ | $\maroonD{3}$

So, we are looking for a $\blueE{\text{total number of services}}$ that we can also $\purpleE{\text{divide by 3}}$ to get $\maroonD{3}$.

|Total||Ratio part
-|-|-|-
Nail trims | $15$ | $\stackrel{\purpleE{\div 3}}\rightarrow$ | $5$
? service | $\blueE{9}$ | $\stackrel{\purpleE{\div 3}}\rightarrow$ | $\maroonD{3}$

$\blueE{9} \purpleE{\div 3} = \maroonD{3}$

What service did the groomer do $9$ times?
For every $5$ nail trims, the groomer gave $3$ baths.

assessment_items.question_content:
Mabel spends $4$ hours to edit a $3$-minute long video. She edits at a constant rate.

 **How long does Mabel spend to edit a $15$-minute long video?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] hours
hints:
Mabel spends the same amount of time editing each minute of video. 

So an equivalent ratio can show us how long it takes to edit $15$ minutes of video.

To make an equivalent ratio, we multiply or divide both values in the first ratio by the same number.

|||
-|-|-|-
Video minutes | $3$ | $\rightarrow$ | $15$
Editing hours | $4$ | $\rightarrow$  | $?$

|||
-|-|-|-
Video minutes | $3$ | $\stackrel{\times \purpleD{5}}\rightarrow$ | $15$
Editing hours | $4$ | $\stackrel{\times \purpleD{5}}\rightarrow$  | $\tealE{20}$

Mabel is editing a video $\purpleD{5}$ times as long, so she spends $\purpleD{5}$ times as long to edit it.
Mabel spends $\tealE{20}$ hours to edit a $15$-minute long video.

assessment_items.question_content:
Elton is a candle maker. Each $15 \text{ cm}$ long candle he makes burns evenly for $6$ hours.

 **If Elton makes a $45 \text{ cm}$ long candle, how long would it burn?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] hours
hints:
The candles burn an equal amount each hour.

So an equivalent ratio can show us how long it takes to burn a $45\,\text{cm}$ candle.

To make an equivalent ratio, we multiply or divide both values in the first ratio by the same number.

|||
-|-|-|-
$\text{cm}$ | $15$ | $\rightarrow$ | $45$
Hours | $6$ | $\rightarrow$  | $?$

|||
-|-|-|-
$\text{cm}$ | $15$ | $\stackrel{\times \purpleD{3}}\rightarrow$ | $45$
Hours | $6$ | $\stackrel{\times \purpleD{3}}\rightarrow$  | $\tealE{18}$

The $45 \text{ cm}$ long candle is $\purpleD{3}$ times as long, so it would burn for $\purpleD{3}$ times as long.
A $45 \text{ cm}$ long candle would burn for $\tealE{18}$ hours.

assessment_items.question_content:
Bao can eat $12$ chicken wings in $3$ minutes. She eats the chicken wings at a constant rate.

 **How many chicken wings can Bao eat in $12$ minutes?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] chicken wings
hints:
Bao eats the same number of chicken wings each minute.

So an equivalent ratio can show us how many chicken wings Bao eats in $12$ minutes.

To make an equivalent ratio, we multiply or divide both values in the first ratio by the same number.

|||
-|-|-|-
Minutes | $3$ | $\rightarrow$ | $12$
Wings | $12$ | $\rightarrow$  | $?$

|||
-|-|-|-
Minutes | $3$ | $\stackrel{\times \purpleD{4}}\rightarrow$ | $12$
Wings | $12$ | $\stackrel{\times \purpleD{4}}\rightarrow$  | $\tealE{48}$

Bao has $\purpleD{4}$ times as many minutes, so she can eat $\purpleD{4}$ times as many chicken wings.
Bao can eat $\tealE{48}$ chicken wings in $12$ minutes.

assessment_items.question_content:
According to Greg, perfect cherry pies have a ratio of $240$ cherries to $3$ pies.

 **How many cherries does Greg need to make $15$ perfect cherry pies?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] cherries
hints:
Greg uses the same number of cherries per pie.

So an equivalent ratio can show us how many cherries we need for $15$ pies.

To make an equivalent ratio, we multiply or divide both values in the first ratio by the same number.

|||
-|-|-|-
Pies | $3$ | $\rightarrow$ | $15$
Cherries | $240$ | $\rightarrow$  | $?$

|||
-|-|-|-
Pies | $3$ | $\stackrel{\times \purpleD{5}}\rightarrow$ | $15$
Cherries | $240$ | $\stackrel{\times \purpleD{5}}\rightarrow$  | $\tealE{1{,}200}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

Greg is making $\purpleD{5}$ times as many pies, so he needs $\purpleD{5}$ times as many cherries.
Greg needs $\tealE{1{,}200}$ cherries to make $15$ perfect cherry pies.

assessment_items.question_content:
Each time the Timberwolves score $1$ touchdown, these things happen.

- The mascot does $4$ back flips.
- The cheerleaders set off $6$ confetti cannons.

**How many touchdowns did the Timberwolves score if the cheerleaders set off $18$ confetti cannons?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] touchdowns
hints:
The cheerleaders set off the same number of confetti cannons per touchdown. 

So an equivalent ratio can show us how many touchdowns it takes to set off $18$ cannons.

To make an equivalent ratio, we multiply or divide both values in the first ratio by the same number.

For every $1$ touchdown, the cheerleaders set off $6$ confetti cannons. The ratio of touchdowns to confetti cannons is $1:6$.

The cheerleaders set off $\redE{18}$ confetti cannons.

|||
-|-|-|-
Touchdowns | $1$ | $\rightarrow$ | $?$
Confetti cannons | $6$ | $\rightarrow$  | $\redE{18}$

Since we multiplied the number of confetti cannons by $3$, we also need to multiply the number of touchdowns by $3$.

|||
-|-|-|-
Touchdowns | $1$ | $\stackrel{\times \purpleD{3}}\rightarrow$ | $\blueE{3}$
Confetti cannons | $6$ | $\stackrel{\times \purpleD{3}}\rightarrow$  | $\redE{18}$
The Timberwolves scored $\blueE{3}$ touchdowns.

assessment_items.question_content:
Finley makes $1$ batch of her favorite shade of orange paint by mixing these paint amounts:

- $5$ liters of yellow paint
- $3$ liters of red paint

**How many batches of orange paint can Finley make if she has $21$ liters of red paint?**  
*Finley has all of the yellow paint that she needs.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
Finley uses the same amount of red paint per batch.

So an equivalent ratio can show us how many batches Finley can  make with $21\,\text{L}$ of red paint.

To make an equivalent ratio, we multiply or divide both values in the first ratio by the same number.

To make $1$ batch of orange paint, Finley uses $3$ liters of red paint. The ratio of batches of orange paint to liters of red paint is $1:3$.

Finley has  $\redE{21\,\text{L}}$ of red paint.

|||
-|-|-|-
Orange paint (batches) | $1$ | $\rightarrow$ | $?$
Red paint ($\text{L}$) | $3$ | $\rightarrow$  | $\redE{21}$

Since Finley has $\purpleD{7}$ times as much red paint, she can make $\purpleD{7}$ times as many batches.

|||
-|-|-|-
Orange paint (batches) | $1$ | $\stackrel{\times \purpleD{7}}\rightarrow$ | $\blueE{7}$
Red paint ($\text{L}$) | $3$ | $\stackrel{\times \purpleD{7}}\rightarrow$  | $\redE{21}$
Finley can make $\blueE{7}$ batches of orange paint.

assessment_items.question_content:
Lucia is making invisibility potions. Each potion requires the following ingredients:

- $2$ eyes of newt
- $5$ carrots
- $3$ liters of rainwater

**How many carrots would Lucia mix with $16$ eyes of newt in the potions?**  
*Lucia has all of the other ingredients she needs.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] carrots
hints:
Lucia uses the same numbers of eyes and carrots per potion.

So an equivalent ratio can show us how many carrots Lucia uses with $16$ eyes.

To make an equivalent ratio, we multiply or divide both values in the first ratio by the same number.

|||
-|-|-|-
Eyes | $2$ | $\rightarrow$ | $16$
Carrots | $5$ | $\rightarrow$  | $?$

|||
-|-|-|-
Eyes | $2$ | $\stackrel{\times \purpleD{8}}\rightarrow$ | $16$
Carrots | $5$ | $\stackrel{\times \purpleD{8}}\rightarrow$  | $\tealE{40}$

Lucia has $\purpleD{8}$ times as many eyes of newt as she needs for $1$ potion, so she needs $\purpleD{8}$ times as many carrots.
Lucia mixes $\tealE{40}$ carrots with $16$ eyes of newt.

assessment_items.question_content:
Atilio is throwing a party. For every $8$ guests, they make the following snacks.

- $20$ fruit skewers
- $16$ popcorn balls
- $4$ liters of milkshake

**If Atilio makes $20$ liters of milkshake, how many fruit skewers do they make?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] fruit skewers
hints:
Atilio makes the same amounts of milkshakes and fruit skewers per guest.

So an equivalent ratio can show us how many fruit skewers Atilio makes with $20\,\text{L}$ of milkshake.

To make an equivalent ratio, we multiply or divide both values in the first ratio by the same number.

|||
-|-|-|-
Milkshake ($\text{L}$) | $4$ | $\rightarrow$ | $20$
Fruit skewers | $20$ | $\rightarrow$ | $?$

|||
-|-|-|-
Milkshake ($\text{L}$) | $4$ | $\stackrel{\times \purpleD{5}}\rightarrow$ | $20$
Fruit skewers | $20$ | $\stackrel{\times \purpleD{5}}\rightarrow$  | $\tealE{100}$

There is $\purpleD{5}$ times as much milkshake, so Atilio makes $\purpleD{5}$ times as many skewers.
Atilio makes $\tealE{100}$ fruit skewers if they make $20\,\text{L}$ of milkshake.
